metadata.title: DarkOnto: An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modeling and Ontology Learning

abstract: Social networks on the dark web are rich in data that provides valuable insight into the nature of the activities on the dark web and human behaviors related to these activities. It also encompasses a diversity of ideologies, interests, and thought patterns associated with illicit activities and businesses on the dark web. For this reason, social networks on the dark web constitute a powerful tool and a profuse data source for various investigative work. However, such investigations encounter considerable challenges related to the massive volumes of textual data, analyzing it effectively, and extracting knowledge from it. This knowledge can be used in various investigations and studies when representing it in ontologies as a unified and integrative data source. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach for extracting and representing knowledge hidden in dark web communities through topic modeling and ontology learning methods. We start from the conceptual design of the ontology and employ several stages of text processing and analysis to achieve the desired knowledge graph, DarkOnto. These stages include data cleaning and preprocessing, topic modeling using correlated topic model (CTM), class-topic similarity estimation, ontology construction, ontology population, and ontology evaluation, where the proposed approach achieved high results. Furthermore, we discuss the results, limitations, challenges, and future work. This paper presents a promising approach for extracting hidden valuable knowledge from dark web communities where investigating and conceptualizing criminal communities can be conducted efficiently.

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: The wide spread of social networks on the web and the continuous development of their technologies has led to exploiting these technologies by diverse segments of the society for various purposes. However, the danger of this spread lies in the use of social networks by individuals and organizations that foster malicious and criminal activities and disseminate their ideologies either to increase the growth of their community or to attract clients and customers to the businesses and services they promote on the dark web. The result is a community of those individuals and groups that may appear to be aimed solely at illicit profit and the exchange of techni-cal knowledge and practical experience, but under which lies a real community that calls for solidarity, cultural and emotional cohesion, and the attraction of like-mindedness and consensus among them.

On the other hand, the rapid spread of crimes based on modern technologies is an obstacle for law enforcement agencies and investigators to persevere with the massive flow of malicious data, in addition to the differences in content evaluation and lacks of language capabilities and expertise required to examine such activities. Crime in all its forms keeps pace with the fast and increasing development of computer technologies, communication means, and social networking facilities, exploiting them to expand criminal networks and illicit activities, from terrorism to organized crime, illicit gain networks, and Crime as a Service (CaaS) [1].

In particular, marketplaces on the dark web, or cryptomarkets, and their associated forums represent robust interactive "Communities of Practice" that seek to build their internal knowledge and play an important supportive role for cryptomarkets in terms of sharing up-to-date information about products, market conditions, vendors, and exchange of knowledge about security tips and safe trading practices on the dark web [2,3].

To keep pace with the massiveness of data size and flow, content analysis helps investigate the correlations within the content of social networks to understand the formation of the community in terms of intellectual components [4]. Content analysis may include several computational techniques to analyze the content of crime, such as Big Data techniques, linguistic and semantic analysis techniques, and advanced modeling [1].

Understanding the criminal mind is a significant aspect of crime analysis. For this purpose, we propose an approach for content analysis of discussions on dark web social networks and constructing an intellectual ontology as a unified resource for investigation systems. The presented approach employs semantic analysis of social networks through topic modeling and ontology construction through ontology learning (OL) techniques to build a knowledge graph, Dar-kOnto, which depicts the common ideologies and concepts shared by the promoters of malicious and illegal activities on cryptomarkets and helps inferring the cultural, psychological, and emotional factors.
The contributions of this paper are as follows:

1. Employing semantic analysis techniques (topic modeling) on social networks on the dark web to extract the latent semantic relationships in dark web discussions 2. Constructing an ontology from the extracted topics, representing the set of interconnected information into a knowledge graph based on the constructed ontology 3. Creating different scenarios for different users and purposes that utilize the resulting knowledge graph to depict and infer the ideologies and concepts that help understanding the intellectual formation of a dark web community Several approaches have been proposed to analyze and extract disseminated concepts on social networks on the dark web. However, there is still no comprehensive study that includes all the conceptual facets of these communities and contributes to extracting ideologies from all technical, social, and emotional aspects and representing them in a network of interconnected concepts. To the best of our knowledge, this research is the first study on semantic content analysis, OL, and cognitive representation of social networks on the dark web.

The remaining of the paper is divided as follows. Section 2 presents related work in three aspects, analyzing social net-works on the dark web, semantic social network analysis (SSNA), and OL. Section 3 introduces a comprehensive background on the dark web, social networks, semantic analysis of social networks, semantic web, OL, knowledge graph as ontologies, and ontology evaluation. Section 4 demonstrates the methodology that includes conceptual design, proposed approach, dataset, data cleaning and preprocessing, topic modeling, class-topic similarity estimation, ontology construction, ontology population, and ontology evaluation. Section 5 presents the discussion of the results. Section 6 presents the limitations and future work. Section 7 is the conclusion.

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: Analyzing Social Networks on the Dark Web. Social networks on the dark web gained increasing interest of researchers for a variety of purposes. Studies in this field included analyzing interactions of a cryptomarket community with events related to their market, such as closure operations conducted by law enforcement [5], and exploring the factors of trust, logistics, and conflict in a cryptomarket community [6]. Other studies focused on the impact of the technical and motivational aspects of cryptomarkets on the growth of their community and the continuity of this evolution by utilizing different models, such as the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) [7] and TOR-use Motivation Model (TMM) [8].

2.2. Semantic SNA. Topic modeling has been widely utilized in analyzing social networks semantically. Studies used Topic modeling to build a vocabulary-based foundation for incremental learning ontology [9], simplifying the graph of a community of practice and detecting key members and topics [10] and real-time semantic analysis of social networks integrated with Linked Open Data (LOD) sources to explore users' interests, values, and most discussed topics [11]. Other studies analyzed concepts circulating on a social network in a form of "viewpoints" using the iterative rank difference (IRD) method [12]. Studies also integrated community detection methods and collaborative filtering techniques to discover topical themes and mine user interests [13] and studied users' preferences across multiple social networks to help understand users' motives for using those networks [14].

Other methods, such as clustering and Louvain methods, were employed to discover natural topics and artificial topics and the community around the detected topics in a graph [15]. GCN-CNN model was proposed to improve text modeling as a graph with statistical and semantic relationships, employing the distributed semantic relationship hypothesis, which states that words that appear in a similar context may share a semantic relationship [16].

Specifically, a concept is defined as a word and its adjacent words in a network, which is constructed by determining the scope and representing it with the network properties [17]. Keyword networks were analyzed to infer prominent topics using an ontology-based framework and a predefined domain-specific ontology (UniDM) to label topics [18]. On the other hand, short texts were modeled through semantically related long texts, following the Metamodel Enabled Latent Dirichlet Association (MELDA), to solve the problem of data scattering [9]. Some studies relied on ontologies and knowledge bases to detect extremism on social networks using corresponding semantics in DBpedia and various classifiers [19]. A framework based on the adaptive knowledge retrieval approach was proposed to provide information to investigators about improvised explosive devices, capitalizing on user-written descriptions of relevant objects, such as tags, which produce user-made classifications, or the so-called folksonomy, to generate semantic maps [20].

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: Topic modeling was variously used as an OL methodology. For example, an ontology can be constructed as a hierarchy of topics, where edges indicate the similarity degree top-down [21]. Studies used a variety of topic models, such as LSI-SVD and Mr.latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA), with the cosine of topic pointwise mutual information (CosTMI) for semantic closeness between topics, while latent topics are semantically clustered using hierarchical agglomerative clustering (HAC) [22]. In another study, a terminological ontology was constructed using a proposed hrLDA model based on LDA to extract hierarchical relationships between noun phrases [23]. Another terminological ontology was constructed using the unigram tagger annotator from the NLTK package to reduce errors in annotations, and a vocabulary intersection was conducted to create compound noun phrases [24].

Other studies employed clustering and classification methods to extract terms and their relationships, such as decision trees, Bayesian K-mean, automated entity relationship (AER) [25], K-means, K-medoids, affinity propagation, DB-scan, coclustering [26], and lexical and morphological analyses to compensate for the shortcomings of clustering algorithms along with LSI model, named entity recognition, and logical inference methods [27]. Furthermore, LDA was used and fed with a seed set of core concepts to classify the terms and enhance the semantic incoherence of clustering [28]. LDA and PLSA were used to construct a topical ontology and enrich the graph of the domain ontology with minimal human intervention relying on a predefined core ontology [29]. The Louvain method was also used to split the graph representation of words into communities, relying on the concept of modularity to measure the quality of the communities [30].

Annotations were used to facilitate extracting the ontology components, such as defining the high-frequency terms that will form the categories and determining a set of key phrasal verbs [31]. OL methods can take advantage of pretrained language models to reduce the size of training data, eliminate the ambiguities of the different meanings of the terms, differentiate between specific and general terms [32], or extend existing ontologies, leading to acquiring new knowledge [33].

In cybercrime, OL was considered to construct a new ontology for classifying cybercrime and integrating or extending previously developed cybercrime classification schemes in multiple perspectives to provide a more holistic view of cybercrime classification [34]. Another study employed text mining and semantic reasoning to compose a knowledge base for crime investigation purposes, integrating it with the Unified Cybersecurity Ontology (UCO) for enrichment [1].

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: The dark web provides the ability to hide user identity, network traffic, and data, and users outside of the dark web cannot access it using standard web browsers but through special software. Therefore, darknets have become a tool for many malicious and illicit purposes, such as drug trafficking, hacking, child exploitation, counterfeit items and weapons trade, and dissemination of extremism content [35]. Researchers consider the dark web as a vital host for various criminal activities. For example, marketplaces on the dark web, or cryptomarkets, are evidence of CaaS [36].

Cryptomarkets are widespread platforms for illegal earning on the dark web, harboring a variety of promoted and traded illicit products and services. The technical sophistication of dark websites makes them unique in terms of operability and hard to track down by law enforcement. Moreover, behind this complex technical aspect lies a real community of members of the cryptomarkets supportive of each other and of the community to which they belong [5,37,38].

The importance of studying social networks associated with these markets lies in understanding the growth of such communities that expand into the dark web, where anonymous individuals gather to share various knowledge related to both their trade and their community. This aspect can provide an understanding of some factors of organized crime, which is increasingly shifting from the real world to the virtual world [39].

Social networks on the dark web, especially forums, provide support systems for information exchange, communication, and experience sharing, as well as providing guides and instructions. The anonymity attracts users to participate in forums without the fear of revealing sensitive information that could lead to social stigma or self-incrimination [40].

Researchers demonstrated that social networks on the dark web involve the emotional attraction that cryptomarkets represent to both vendors and consumers, particularly the secretive nature of their virtual personalities [41] and especially those who have liberal ideals and believe in creating a space to experience personal freedom [42]. Cryptomarkets have generated openness for their members to discuss illegal business that users outside these markets cannot perform. In addition, despite the ongoing shutdowns implemented by law enforcement agencies on many cryptomarkets that threaten their confidence in the possibility of lasting freedom, their desire to rebuild this possibility elsewhere on the dark web is still increasing [42]. This aspect of social networking on the dark web demonstrates the existence of actual communities that go beyond the technical discussions and exchange of experiences, exemplified by robust standards of unity, support, and mutual interactions that sometimes display high energy of empathy. It also includes social subjects, which can be considered a means of social mingling, which may express their desire for a community of their own [5]. Analyzing social networks on the dark web can reveal significant perspectives on crime and organized crime [43].

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: Networks. An online social network can be defined as a network of social relationships between individuals in a virtual environment on the Internet, where they share their interests, activities, and viewpoints and obtain information and disseminate it to a wide range [43]. Therefore, social media is considered an enormous set of information that provides insights into online communities and constitutes a rich source of behavioral evidence for their members [11].

Nowadays, millions of users use these platforms as new communication tools where they create their virtual profiles and communicate with each other regardless of geographic locations and physical restrictions, in addition to using them as instant dynamic data sources [44]. Furthermore, the ease and speed of access to digital media lead to a dramatic and rapid increase in the volume of data generated by these media. Remarkably, most of this data is textual, as people from various interests write articles and diverse posts on websites, forums, social networks, blogs, emails, and other services [15].

Social networks allow users to connect for conceptual and emotional thinking, sharing content and advertisements, disseminating information, or simply socializing online. What differentiates them from traditional web pages is that users are not just consumers of information but also a diversity of content creators. Therefore, the contents of a social network can be considered a dynamic reflection of the emotional and societal fluctuations of its members [43] with an impact on forming diverse viewpoints on particular topics with the ease of expressing them [12]. Moreover, knowledge is shared through blogging services or forums, where users post their questions, answers, and suggestions to develop joint work in communities of practice [10].

However, the raw information on social networks suffers from the lack of a clear structure and the diversity of its patterns, which makes its analysis difficult without considering appropriate measures. Therefore, computational methods are essential to convert unstructured textual content into structured data that is understandable by the machine to analyze [45].

SNA is an interdisciplinary research field that combines social sciences and computational sciences [46]. SNA can be concerned with the statistical, graphical, and social characteristics of social networks. Thus, it can be divided into three areas: static analysis, dynamic analysis, and semantic analysis [43]. The latter is our focus in this research.

3.3. Semantic Analysis of Social Networks. Semantic analysis of social networks helps discover relationships within the semantics of members' discussions and posts, highlighting the content interdependence within the community [4]. The semantic analysis process comprises extracting grammatical and semantic relationships between entities in the text to form information [47].

Furthermore, Semantic Web frameworks provide models for graphic representation (such as Resource Description Framework [RDF]), schema definition (such as Resource Description Framework Schema [RDFS] and Web Ontology Language [OWL]), and a query language (such as SPARQL Protocol and RDF Query Language [SPARQL]) for representing and querying social data. Moreover, visualization is essential in semantic analysis to depict the results through a graph, representing the various aspects of the social network contents [43].

3.4. Semantic Web. The Semantic Web arose from the need to find semantic similarities in texts in search and query operations so that they express the same meaning in different words [43]. In 2001, a vision of the Semantic Web was presented as a leap forward from the traditional web, called Web 3.0 or Web of Data, which is based on employing formal resources to provide a common vocabulary and semantically link web data in a machine-readable format [22].

The Semantic Web techniques explain web resources with semantic information, provide a common understanding of web data and make it shareable, and provide interoperability between applications through common knowledge resources. These resources are the ontologies [48,49].

The term "Ontology" refers to the vocabularies and the concepts they express [50]. Through ontologies, the Semantic Web provides in-depth information with its ability to organize and structure content and provide machine readability and resource reusability [22]. Subsequently, OL helps knowledge engineers to construct their ontology for a specific knowledge domain [22,25], and the success of these knowledge bases depends on the quality of the underlying ontologies [49], ensuring low cost of effort and time [51].

Consequently, Ontology can be defined as a formal and explicit definition of a particular knowledge domain. It is an abstract knowledge model that handles knowledge as a set of concepts, their associated attributes, and the relationships among them [52]. These ontological models characterize linguistic terms and their underlying concepts. Furthermore, ontologies study the semantics existing in the real world, and the semantics are formal and explicit properties of a shared concept [22,53]. In addition, presenting an efficient ontological study for a specific domain is the first stage to creating an effective system to represent the domain knowledge and provide the possibility to share this knowledge base in various applied fields [25].

Modeling knowledge by ontologies allows it to be represented formally, as well as to reason and infer new knowledge. Therefore, it is considered a solution to the web's lack of formal semantics and the massive and accelerating increase in the volume of information on the web without an explicit conceptual perception [54].

On the other hand, conceptualization is defined as the depiction of a given domain of knowledge concerning the theories and connections between domain entities and facilitating domain analysis. The concept is not just a term describing a cognitive entity but rather a description of the entity, as it defines the entity's correlations with other entities within a set of rules that apply to all of the occurrences of the entity within the group [25]. In short, the concept is the foundational element of an ontology [27].

Ontologies provide a more flexible and distributed approach to data representation. Studies have also demonstrated that ontology-based methods have better accuracy than traditional methods in miscellaneous tasks such as text clustering, link prediction, information extraction, information retrieval, recommendation systems, artificial intelligence, and language translation [21,43,46]. Studies also show that ontology-based semantic search achieves more accuracy and allows vocabulary demystification [11].
The advantages of ontologies can be summarized in the following points [25,31,49,50,55,56]:
• Ontologies provide a common vocabulary for a given knowledge domain by conceptualizing and structuring the knowledge
• Ontologies provide a common understanding among individual, organizations, and systems and bridge cognitive gaps in understanding the knowledge domain
• Ontologies separate domain knowledge from operational knowledge
• Ontologies provide semantic interoperability between heterogeneous systems with high accuracy
• Ontology metadata facilitate integrating and extending the ontology

• Ontologies provide a solution for language ambiguity, where several different terms can refer to the same concept, by explicitly describing concepts and their relationships and providing a widely accepted and consistent vocabulary
• Ontologies unify vocabularies or semantics with the ability to control them to infer declarative knowledge
• Ontologies provide core concepts as a reference to describe other concepts
• Ontologies define homogeneous terms that help eliminate contradictions
• Ontologies provide a visual representation of knowledge
• The formality provided by ontologies enables knowledge structures to be integrated, organized, linked, exchanged, and reused and supports better computational inferences
• Ontologies allow automatic aggregation, practical use, and reuse of distributed heterogeneous data sources
• Ontology modules can be rearranged or reorganized
• Rules can be added to ontologies to infer new knowledge
The Semantic Web mainly consists of four technologies [43,46,57]:

1. RDF: It is a Semantic Web standard developed to represent decentralized machine-interpretable knowledge built on a unified formal vocabulary and graph-based data model rich in formal semantics and interpretations. RDF is a standardized model definition for representing and defining resources (the things that are described), properties (relationships and associations among things), and classes (categories used to group things). An RDF statement is called a triple and has the form <subject, predicate, object>, where the subject refers to a unique resource, the object refers to either a unique or literal resource, and the predicate represents the relationship between the subject and object. This triple can be thought of as a directed edge between two nodes (resources), and a set of these nodes and edges form a directed graph.
The RDF triple database is called the Triplestore.
Based on graph theory, the RDF data model represents the core of a labeled directed multigraph through a set of RDF statements.

2. RDFS: An extension of the RDF frameworks that allow describing classes and properties, defining relationships between subclasses and superclasses, and providing a vocabulary for modeling the semantics of resources embedded in RDF data and customizing datatype definitions.

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: A semantic language designed to represent rich and intricate knowledge about semantic relationships. OWL adds semantics to the schema and expands the vocabulary of semantic modeling to express more complex classes, properties, and relationships by defining the fundamentals of the new language. In other words, RDFS is built on RDF, and OWL is built on RDFS.

paragraphs: A standard query language for matching patterns in RDF graphs. It is used to query required and optional graph patterns with relationships that connect or separate them.

Mathematically, a graph consists of two basic units: vertices and edges. Each edge is defined by a pair of vertices, also called endpoints. Thus, a graph G consists of a nonempty set of vertices V G and a set of edges E G , defining the graph as G = V G , E G . The order of the graph G is represented by the total number of vertices n or V G = n, while the size of the graph is the total number of edges E G = m [58].

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: With the massive growth of information, the need to integrate ontology into applications and information systems of specific knowledge domains increases [50]. However, constructing an ontology manually takes exhaustive time and effort, especially with the exponential growth of concepts in the real world. Moreover, manual ontology construction suffers from several issues. It is costly of effort and time, prone to errors, difficult to maintain, developer biased, and inflexible and requires extensive knowledge of a specific domain. Therefore, studies are directed increasingly towards developing computational learning methods and tools to automate or semi-automate ontology construction to keep pace with the rapid evolution of information and reduce time and human effort [49,51,54,59]. These methodologies and techniques are called OL techniques [21].

OL is defined as the process of extracting conceptual knowledge from the input and producing an ontology as the output [49]. It can also be defined as a set of methods and techniques used to build an ontology from scratch or to enrich or adapt an existing ontology in an automated or semiautomated manner from several heterogeneous data sources [54], and it can produce ontologies with higher complexity [60].

OL is concerned with automatic or semi-automatic information extraction from a set of texts, including extracting terms and concepts and building hierarchies of the extracted concepts and the relationships connecting them [21]. Moreover, lexicosemantic relations express a comprehensive knowledge structure that constitutes the fundamental cognitive functions between the extracted concepts and represents the essence of any conceptual representation of the corpus [53]. Ontology based on concepts extracted from the text is called concept hierarchy or terminological ontology [22]. Terminological ontologies can be used to map documents and corresponding terms to the same domain, which helps understand other related documents and their semantics and leads to incremental learning. Such ontologies can significantly reduce the cost of time and human efforts in ontology engineering practices [29].

OL methodologies are classified according to the type of inputs used in learning, which may be unstructured, semistructured, or structured, such as ontologies constructed from text, dictionary, knowledge base, semistructured schemas, and relational schemas [54].

For a textual corpus, the advantage of utilizing OL techniques is that the construction process does not need a seed ontology but only a set of texts [21]. Moreover, studies demonstrate the significant advantages of OL in constructing and managing affluent knowledge bases and enriching semantic retrieval [22].

An OL methodology can include several computational methods. They can be statistical, linguistic, heuristic, logicbased, pattern matching, machine learning, or a hybrid of a number of these techniques. These methods are used when constructing an ontology from scratch for their proven effectiveness in integrating concept definition and relationship extraction processes [29,49,51].

One of the promising approaches for automatic ontology construction is based on probabilistic topic modeling. Topic modeling relies on information theory to extract topics and their relationships from texts [21]. Consequently, topic modeling has become a trend recently in OL, as they are unsupervised algorithms that have proven advantageous in understanding the content of a large corpus [29]. From each topic, a set of the most relevant terms are extracted according to the probability distribution. The extracted terms are the most likely terms representing a topic and are used to classify the clusters [28].

Furthermore, annotations are applied to the discovered terms of similar semantics to name the concept with extra semantically similar labels, thus producing a set of basic concepts with their possible synonyms, with a set of the main types of relationships among them [31].
The most common approach to depict OL steps is the "Ontology Layer Cake" shown in Figure 1 [26,59].
These stages are defined as follows [61]:

• Term extraction: The essential stage for all aspects of OL from text, as the term constitutes the foundational unit of constructing the ontology. A term can be a multiword or a single-word token that describes a specific meaning within a given domain.

• Synonym discovery: It aims to find terms that describe the same concept and appear in the same group of a given concept. It is possible to use available synonym groups, clustering techniques, or others.
• Concept formation: In general, the concept includes intention, extension, and lexical connotations used to describe the concepts (terms).

• Concept hierarchy: It is the main structure of the constructed ontology, and it includes the process of producing, refining, and expanding concepts and is aimed at building a hierarchical taxonomy of concepts. • Relation discovery: It is aimed at extracting a new relationship between known concepts.
• Rule/axiom extraction: It is aimed at inferring rules based on the extracted concepts and relationships.

To accomplish these stages accurately, linguistic techniques are crucial for proper data preprocessing, such as language properties analysis, grammatical structure analysis, dependency analysis within sentences, lexical patterns, and taxonomic and nontaxonomic relationship extraction [59].

In particular, extracting the nontaxonomic relationship includes two tasks: defining the relations connecting concepts (usually a verb) and expressing the relationship, which consists of two concepts and the title of the relationship between them [27].
OL methodologies can be categorized according to several criteria [22]:
• The type of knowledge resources for which the ontology is required (structured, semistructured, or unstructured data)
• The level of automation (fully automated or semiautomated)
• The learning objective (concepts, taxonomy, conceptual relationships, attributes, instances, or axioms)
• The purpose (creating or updating an ontology)
• The learning techniques (linguistics, statistical, or hybrid)

Corpus-based OL proved effective in solving diverse problems in a short time. Therefore, the constructed ontologies can be integrated into various data-driven applications, data models, search engines, and others [32].

OL methodologies face significant challenges due to the rapid increase in unstructured data on the web, making automated OL from text one of the most crucial research areas. OL combines various techniques from different fields, such as machine learning, text mining, knowledge representation, reasoning, information retrieval, and natural language processing, to introduce a certain level of automation in constructing an ontology from unstructured text [49].

3.6. Knowledge Graphs as Ontologies. A knowledge graph is generally defined as a set of interconnected information usually limited to a specific knowledge domain and managed as a graph. These interrelationships between information provide new insights into the desired domain. A knowledge graph describes real-world entities and their interrelationships, identifies potential categories and relationships between entities in a schema, allows entities to be linked, and can cover different subject areas [62].

The knowledge gained from the ontologies can be used to annotate web pages, customize and generalize concepts, integrate data by providing a common schema, assist intelligent search engines in search methods, and facilitate query response and logical inference from different data sources by employing the relationships between existing concepts in the ontology. Ontology is the tool that allows control of how the corresponding knowledge bases are populated with instances by a process known as the ontology population or knowledge markup by which knowledge bases can be created from ontologies [29,51].

Using ontology as a knowledge base helps validate semantic associations and draw conclusions from known facts. Therefore, researchers suggest that ontology is not different from the knowledge graph, where the ontology can also include instances and not just categories and characteristics, while others indicate that the knowledge graph outweighs the ontology in terms of size and additional features that it provides, and it can be described as a massive ontology [25].

Knowledge-based modeling and processing can be divided into four categories: (a) ontology, (b) cognitive knowledge base, (c) linguistic knowledge base, and (d) expert knowledge base. Ontology-based knowledge is represented by sets of concepts with corresponding attributes, values, and relationships [52]. 7 Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies 3.7. Ontology Evaluation. The goal of ontology evaluation is to validate the accuracy of ontology components (vocabulary, concepts, and relationships) and to ensure that the constructed ontology is adequate for the defined domain [60].
The 2013 Ontology Summit proposed the life cycle evaluation approach, which states that an ontology should be evaluated throughout its development and usage lifecycle [50].

McDaniel and Storey suggest a path to ontology validation and improvement represented by Figure 2, which can be used as a whole, part of it, or a combination of several methodologies to achieve higher accuracy [50].
These stages are described as follows [50]:
1. Validation phase:
• Check fitness: It includes methods to check the suitability of the ontology for a specific task or domain, such as matching the ontology with the objectives or user requirements.
• Remove errors: Errors must be eliminated to ensure the usability of the ontology. This stage includes the removal of syntactical errors, checking for inconsistencies, and reducing redundancies.
• Apply metrics: Quality metrics are applied to determine which ontologies are the highest quality, if there is more than one, based on criteria selected by the user, such as using a ranking system.

paragraphs: • Pruning and modularization: It is used when the application needs only a fraction of the complete ontology for a particular goal or task.
• Place in a library: It consists of creating or finding an appropriate repository for the ontology that can be managed, maintained, easy to access, sharable, and searchable.
Ontology quality measures can be classified into four categories according to the desired type of ontology and the purpose of the evaluation [56,59]:

• Gold-standard-based evaluation: It is based on a predefined gold-standard ontology and can efficiently verify the consistency of the ontology and its domain coverage by comparing it with the standard ontology.

Data (i) Texts of discussions from dark web forum Cleaner (i) Textual data cleaning and preprocessing (ii) Generating corpus Topic modeling (i) N-gram (bi-gram) extraction (ii) Generating new corpus (iii) CTM topic model (iv) Topic coherence measure (v) Select top 50 frequent words for each topic Concept-term similarity estimation (i) Calculate similarities (ii) Assign top three topics to each concept (iii) Assign top three concepts to each topic (iv) Assign terms to concepts Ontology construction (i) Construct ontology scheme (ii) Populate ontology (iii) Add concept relationships Ontology evaluation (i) Verification (ii) Validation Ideology Concept Term In ideology Involve term Has top concept Related to concept Figure 3: Abstract representation of the classes ideology, concept, and term and their relationships.

Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies Techniques based on gold standards are also known as ontology mapping or ontology alignment. However, this evaluation may not be possible if the gold standard is not available or if it is of low quality.

• Application-based or task-based evaluation: It evaluates the produced ontology by employing it in a particular application or conducting specific tasks. It helps to discover inconsistent concepts and evaluates the adaptability of a particular ontology by analyzing its performance in the context of the tasks performed. However, it cannot verify the appropriateness of the content and design of the ontology.

• Data-driven or corpus-based evaluation: It employs existing domain-specific knowledge sources to assess the extent to which the resulting ontology covers the concepts of a given domain and verifies the completeness, brevity, and accuracy of the ontology. However, it does not verify the validity, clarity, or usefulness of the ontology.

• Human evaluation: Also called criteria-based evaluation. It depends on defining and formulating criteria for selecting the adequate ontology from a specific set of candidate ontologies, where a score is assigned to each ontology against each criterion, and then, a weighted sum of scores is calculated for each ontology.

It is considered the most sufficient evaluation where the quality of the acquired ontology is directly proportional to human intervention. Thus, a semiautomated ontology acquisition process often produces a good quality ontology. This assessment may include several criteria for clarity, completeness, and coverage and verifies the content and design of the ontology.

Metric-based or computational evaluation is favorable over human-based assessment for its objectivity. Metricbased techniques determine the quality of a particular fea-ture of an ontology and allow initial adjustments during the ontology design process without waiting for the ontology to reach its final form. Nevertheless, it requires iterative experiments to prove that the applied measures are valid and appropriate [50].

In addition, precision, recall, and F-measure can be used to estimate the effectiveness of the concepts and relationship extraction process. Precision refers to the accuracy of the extracted concepts or relationships, recall refers to the completeness of the extracted concepts or relationships, and the F-measure is a weighted harmonic average between precision and recall. These measures are defined in Equations ( 1), (2), and (3), respectively [63]:

concepts or relations total extracted concepts or relations = true positive true positive + false positive 1 Recall = correct extracted concepts or relations total correct concepts or relations in corpus = true positive true positive + false negative 2 F-measure = 2 * precision * recall precision + recall 3

Data (i) Texts of discussions from dark web forum Cleaner (i) Textual data cleaning and preprocessing (ii) Generating corpus Topic modeling (i) N-gram (bi-gram) extraction (ii) Generating new corpus (iii) CTM topic model (iv) Topic coherence measure (v) Select top 50 frequent words for each topic Concept-term similarity estimation (i) Calculate similarities (ii) Assign top three topics to each concept (iii) Assign top three concepts to each topic (iv) Assign terms to concepts Ontology construction (i) Construct ontology scheme (ii) Populate ontology (iii) Add concept relationships Ontology evaluation (i) Verification (ii) Validation Ideology Concept Term In ideology Involve term Has top concept Related to concept Figure 3: Abstract representation of the classes ideology, concept, and term and their relationships.

8

Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies Techniques based on gold standards are also known as ontology mapping or ontology alignment. However, this evaluation may not be possible if the gold standard is not available or if it is of low quality.

• Application-based or task-based evaluation: It evaluates the produced ontology by employing it in a particular application or conducting specific tasks. It helps to discover inconsistent concepts and evaluates the adaptability of a particular ontology by analyzing its performance in the context of the tasks performed. However, it cannot verify the appropriateness of the content and design of the ontology.

• Data-driven or corpus-based evaluation: It employs existing domain-specific knowledge sources to assess the extent to which the resulting ontology covers the concepts of a given domain and verifies the completeness, brevity, and accuracy of the ontology. However, it does not verify the validity, clarity, or usefulness of the ontology.

• Human evaluation: Also called criteria-based evaluation. It depends on defining and formulating criteria for selecting the adequate ontology from a specific set of candidate ontologies, where a score is assigned to each ontology against each criterion, and then, a weighted sum of scores is calculated for each ontology.

It is considered the most sufficient evaluation where the quality of the acquired ontology is directly proportional to human intervention. Thus, a semiautomated ontology acquisition process often produces a good quality ontology. This assessment may include several criteria for clarity, completeness, and coverage and verifies the content and design of the ontology.

Metric-based or computational evaluation is favorable over human-based assessment for its objectivity. Metricbased techniques determine the quality of a particular fea-ture of an ontology and allow initial adjustments during the ontology design process without waiting for the ontology to reach its final form. Nevertheless, it requires iterative experiments to prove that the applied measures are valid and appropriate [50].

In addition, precision, recall, and F-measure can be used to estimate the effectiveness of the concepts and relationship extraction process. Precision refers to the accuracy of the extracted concepts or relationships, recall refers to the completeness of the extracted concepts or relationships, and the F-measure is a weighted harmonic average between precision and recall. These measures are defined in Equations ( 1), (2), and (3), respectively [63]:

concepts or relations total extracted concepts or relations = true positive true positive + false positive 1 Recall = correct extracted concepts or relations total correct concepts or relations in corpus = true positive true positive + false negative 2 F-measure = 2 * precision * recall precision + recall 3

paragraphs: Social ontology describes social scenarios in a community, permanent and changing social structures, intentions, beliefs, desires, and various discussed, shared, proven, or Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies refuted matters within social networks [25]. It is possible to learn from behavioral and social data and represent it as a knowledge base in the form of an ontology, and with the ontology's ability to integrate data from multiple sources, social ontologies help analyze social data on a large scale [64]. For this purpose, OL needs thoughtful decisions about the type of knowledge to be extracted, the applied purpose of constructing the ontology, and the applications that will benefit from the produced knowledge base [49]. In particular, three elements are required to define an ontology as a formal and explicit specification of a shared concept: domain concepts, relationships, and instances [52].

In the following sections, we demonstrate the conceptual design of DarkOnto in Section 4.1, the proposed approach in Section 4.2, dataset in Section 4.3, data cleaning and preprocessing in Section 4.4, topic modeling in Section 4.5, classtopic similarity estimation in Section 4.6, ontology construction in Section 4.7, ontology population in Section 4.8, and ontology evaluation in Section 4.9.

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: Ideology is defined as "a system of concepts and views" [65]. It can also be defined as "a system of ideas and ideals" [66]. In sociology, the term ideology encompasses a wide range of concepts, views, values, beliefs, assumptions, and expectations that shape an individual's actions and interactions. These factors are significantly influenced by the society to which the individual belongs and are reflected in various societal components such as events, statuses, and how the society should be structured and operated [65,67]. As such, ideologies are closely linked to societal structure and economic systems. Additionally, individuals' beliefs about their positions in society, their relationships and responsibilities towards others, and their experiences and interests all contribute to the formation of their ideology, which they may also feel compelled to share with others [65]. Moreover, specific communities, such as those related to customers and consumption in a market, ideologies convey customers' behaviors, thinking, and desires, in addition to actions needed to communicate with others and establish Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies personal meaning and identity in the community of the market, which can be thought of in a consumerism perspective [66].

Projecting the abovementioned definitions of ideology on a community of a cryptomarket on the dark web, we define three classes (the nouns) for our ontology scheme: ideology, concept, and term. The ideology represents the major category of a group of concepts, the concept represents the thoughts, ideas, and thinking patterns found in the forum, and the term is the class of all terms extracted from the forum discussions grouped by one or more concepts.
To compose a graph of concepts based on this design, the generated graph will be constructed with three types of connections (the verbs):
1. Linked concepts to terms 2. Linked concepts to ideologies 3. Linked concepts to concepts Consequently, we define the relationships between these three classes as follows:

1. In ideology: Defines the specific ideology under which each defined concept lies. In other words, for a given concept, there is an ideology to associate with in a relationship defined as ["Concept" is In Ideology "Ideology"]. Therefore, each concept is in only one ideology, but each ideology may cover one or more concepts.

These techniques are needed in the stages of processing the textual data, and they consist of data cleaning and preprocessing, topic modeling, concept-topic similarity estimation, ontology construction, ontology population, and ontology evaluation.

Figure 4 demonstrates the proposed approach with each step in detail. These steps are thoroughly described in the following sections. 4.3. Dataset. We applied our approach on forum discussions of the Wallstreet cryptomarket. The dataset was retrieved from the AZSecure Dark Net Forums datasets 1 provided by Du et al. [68]. We created our corpus from the flat content of the post, without revealing any identities or usernames.

paragraphs: The textual content of online social networks often includes several issues that make the analysis problematic without prior measures, such as irregular capitalization, emoji, symbols, and accented and special characters, in addition to noise, which is an irregular textual inclusion that affects the accuracy of the analysis, such as unmeaningful words. Therefore, text preprocessing methods are needed to eliminate these issues and prepare the textual data for more proper analyses.

For this purpose, thoughtful steps of text cleaning and preprocessing are conducted on the dark web forum data, described as follows, and depicted in Figure 5. 9. Remove specific words observed through the text but add no meaning to the text, such as "wrote" and "would," and the names of the months (basically before the post declaring the posting date)
The resulting new corpus is stored in a JSON file for the next stage.

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: Text analysis is aimed at identifying the shared characteristics among data points within the text, which identify concepts, or topics, comprised in documents. Thus, topics are represented by the relationships and cooccurrences of terms of the document [69]. One of the leading methods to determine the keywords and relationships among terms is topic modeling. In topic modeling, terms are the primary units of the process, and top keywords describe the fundamental purpose of the text [30].

Words do not just co-occur; they have semantic relations. Topic modeling identifies patterns of speech, discussions, and thoughts and organizes, understands, and summarizes texts, resulting in document annotation according to the extracted topics. Therefore, we employ topic modeling for its ability to capture the semantic dependency between words in the document and to model text as feature graphs with semantic relationships. We analyze concepts with two different criteria: keywords and key phrases (bi-gram).

For topic modeling, we employ the correlated topic model (CTM) [70]. CTM is a probabilistic topic modeling method based on the LDA [71]. The advantage of CTM is that it captures the semantic correlations among concepts using the logistic normal distribution [69]. Therefore, it extracts the latent topics within a corpus along with their relationships, in a form of a network of semantically connected topics.

CTM employs the Bag-of-Words (BoWs) model to analyze explicitly occurring words in the documents. However, BoW does not capture the relations among lexically and semantically connected terms [43]. Therefore, we use Ngram to overcome the shortcomings of the BoW model.
For the weighing scheme, we use the pointwise mutual information (PMI), which consider the terms' dependencies and co-occurrences [72].
We implemented the N-gram corpus generator and CTM topic model using Tomotopy 3 Python package for topic modeling. Figure 6 illustrates the followed steps for this stage.

We feed the corpus generated in the previous stage to the N-grams corpus generator. We define the candidate selection criterion with minimum corpus frequency min cf = 20, minimum document frequency min df = 2, and maximum number of grams max len = 2 4 . Furthermore, the selected bi-grams are concatenated with an underscore (_) to be treated as one word later in the topic model process. Finally, a new corpus is generated with the original terms and the added bi-gram terms.

Next, we load the new corpus to the CTM model, defining the model with the PMI weighing scheme, and the number of generated topics k. We train the model using Gibbs sampling with 30 iterations for the sampling process. Each iteration implies 20 cross-sampling iterations, which makes a total of 600 iterations. The total number of documents on which the model was trained is 45,342 documents, extracting a total of 1,672,502 terms, and using 122,245 vocabularies after filtering the terms with the term weighing measure.

We selected the optimal number of iterations where it reaches the lower perplexity [73]. We test the model with six values of k: 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, and 35. To select the optimal number of topics, we apply CNPMI coherence measure [74]. Table 1 presents the CNPMI value for each value of k, where the higher value indicates the best coherence; in this case, k = 10 is the optimal number of topics.

Consequently, 10 topics are extracted and the topic relation network is generated. For each topic, we select the Top 50 frequent terms to describe the topic. We visualize the topic map using pyLDAvis 5 showing the sizes of the topic, demonstrated in Figure 7.

For the topic correlations, we choose the positive correlations indicating that two words from two different topics are likely to appear in the same topic [70]. Table 2 presents the correlation values between the pairs of topics. Figure 8 illustrates the topic network. 4.6. Class-Topic Similarity Estimation. To form the concepts and their correlations, the numerical IDs of the topics must be transformed into meaningful labels. Depending on the extracted terms, we define the labels for the generated topics to be the concepts and then define the ideology classes according to the defined concepts. Moreover, we use our satisfaction factor [75] to define ontology classes and topic labels.

For the concept class, we found 16 concepts related to the extracted topical terms. The 16 concepts with direct relations to the topical terms are consumer mind, counterfeit items, drugs, emotion, encryption, knowledge sharing, mar-keting, networking, opinion sharing, presentation, profession, profit, satisfaction, security, services, and state of mind. Furthermore, we assign some of these concepts to top-level

Ideology In ideology Term Concept Involve term Related to concept Has top concept 15 Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies concepts for grouping them to broader concepts. Thus, counterfeit items and drugs fall under the products upper level concept, and products and services fall under the offering top-level concept.

sections.text:
Text analysis is aimed at identifying the shared characteristics among data points within the text, which identify concepts, or topics, comprised in documents. Thus, topics are represented by the relationships and cooccurrences of terms of the document [69]. One of the leading methods to determine the keywords and relationships among terms is topic modeling. In topic modeling, terms are the primary units of the process, and top keywords describe the fundamental purpose of the text [30].

Words do not just co-occur; they have semantic relations. Topic modeling identifies patterns of speech, discussions, and thoughts and organizes, understands, and summarizes texts, resulting in document annotation according to the extracted topics. Therefore, we employ topic modeling for its ability to capture the semantic dependency between words in the document and to model text as feature graphs with semantic relationships. We analyze concepts with two different criteria: keywords and key phrases (bi-gram).

For topic modeling, we employ the correlated topic model (CTM) [70]. CTM is a probabilistic topic modeling method based on the LDA [71]. The advantage of CTM is that it captures the semantic correlations among concepts using the logistic normal distribution [69]. Therefore, it extracts the latent topics within a corpus along with their relationships, in a form of a network of semantically connected topics.

CTM employs the Bag-of-Words (BoWs) model to analyze explicitly occurring words in the documents. However, BoW does not capture the relations among lexically and semantically connected terms [43]. Therefore, we use Ngram to overcome the shortcomings of the BoW model.

For the weighing scheme, we use the pointwise mutual information (PMI), which consider the terms' dependencies and co-occurrences [72].

We implemented the N-gram corpus generator and CTM topic model using Tomotopy 3 Python package for topic modeling. Figure 6 illustrates the followed steps for this stage.

We feed the corpus generated in the previous stage to the N-grams corpus generator. We define the candidate selection criterion with minimum corpus frequency min cf = 20, minimum document frequency min df = 2, and maximum number of grams max len = 2 4 . Furthermore, the selected bi-grams are concatenated with an underscore (_) to be treated as one word later in the topic model process. Finally, a new corpus is generated with the original terms and the added bi-gram terms.

Next, we load the new corpus to the CTM model, defining the model with the PMI weighing scheme, and the number of generated topics k. We train the model using Gibbs sampling with 30 iterations for the sampling process. Each iteration implies 20 cross-sampling iterations, which makes a total of 600 iterations. The total number of documents on which the model was trained is 45,342 documents, extracting a total of 1,672,502 terms, and using 122,245 vocabularies after filtering the terms with the term weighing measure.

We selected the optimal number of iterations where it reaches the lower perplexity [73]. We test the model with six values of k: 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, and 35. To select the optimal number of topics, we apply CNPMI coherence measure [74]. Table 1 presents the CNPMI value for each value of k, where the higher value indicates the best coherence; in this case, k = 10 is the optimal number of topics.

Consequently, 10 topics are extracted and the topic relation network is generated. For each topic, we select the Top 50 frequent terms to describe the topic. We visualize the topic map using pyLDAvis 5 showing the sizes of the topic, demonstrated in Figure 7.

For the topic correlations, we choose the positive correlations indicating that two words from two different topics are likely to appear in the same topic [70]. Table 2 presents the correlation values between the pairs of topics. Figure 8 illustrates the topic network. 4.6. Class-Topic Similarity Estimation. To form the concepts and their correlations, the numerical IDs of the topics must be transformed into meaningful labels. Depending on the extracted terms, we define the labels for the generated topics to be the concepts and then define the ideology classes according to the defined concepts. Moreover, we use our satisfaction factor [75] to define ontology classes and topic labels.

For the concept class, we found 16 concepts related to the extracted topical terms. The 16 concepts with direct relations to the topical terms are consumer mind, counterfeit items, drugs, emotion, encryption, knowledge sharing, mar-keting, networking, opinion sharing, presentation, profession, profit, satisfaction, security, services, and state of mind. Furthermore, we assign some of these concepts to top-level

Ideology In ideology Term Concept Involve term Related to concept Has top concept 15 Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies concepts for grouping them to broader concepts. Thus, counterfeit items and drugs fall under the products upper level concept, and products and services fall under the offering top-level concept.

After defining the classes of concepts, we rank the generated topics according to a class-term similarity estimation to detect the most relevant topic to the class label. For this purpose, we use spaCy 6 similarity function. The class-topic similarity estimator works as follows:

1. Loading the English core NLP model en_core_web_lg 7 provided by spaCy 2. Reading the Excel file containing the topics' terms 3. Convert topic column to a term list 4. Read each term and calculate its similarity with each defined label using the similarity function 5. Create lists of terms and their calculations

paragraphs: As a result, the similarity estimator generates 16 CSV files corresponding to the 16 defined classes. Consequently, the similarities are summed for each topic in each file. We assign topics to labels according to two criteria:
• Top three topics with the highest similarities to a class

• Top three classes with the highest similarities to a topic We define these two criteria because some topics are not selected as top three topics for any of the concepts, such as topic T#4, while other topics are overmatched to several concepts, such as topic T#0. Table 3 shows the top three topics for each concept, while Table 4 shows the top three concepts for each topic, and according to the second criterion, the assigned concepts to each topic are demonstrated in Table 5.

Finally, we assign the terms of the selected topics to the class. As a result, we achieve lists of terms included in each class, in other words, a concept and its involved terms. In this process, only a few terms were added manually as they were not well recognized by the dictionary due to their high specificity to the dark web, such as tor, onionshop, pgp, and opsec. 4.7. Ontology Construction. The defined classes and relationships will be the scheme of our ontology, DarkOnto. For simple and fast creation, we use WebVOWL 8 1.1.7 to create the scheme, exported as Turtle file (TTL) for later use. Figure 9 illustrates the classes and relationships as represented by WebVOWL. In this figure, the classes ideology, concept, and term are of type owl:Class. The relations in ideology, involve term, has top concept, and related to concept are of type owl:ObjectProperty. We choose OWL for its ability to represent the defined classes and relationships in a form of triples constructed based on a formal modeling and application of various axioms and logical axioms [76].
We store the created scheme in an Ontotext GraphDB 9 repository for later use.

4.8. Ontology Population. Concepts are not independent but rather have semantic relations connecting them. Therefore, a network of concepts is composed to infer a more generalized concept. In other words, we build a graph of concepts to make the generated graph represent a network of concepts besides the network of keywords and key phrases to understand the relationships among concepts.
We assign each concept to an ideology. We define six ideologies for the ideology class: acquainting, businesses, consuming, emotional, pleasure, and technical.
Table 6 presents the defined ideologies, concepts, and subconcepts.

We create a tabular data sheet that includes all the required mapping data of the ideologies, concepts, and terms. As previously mentioned in Section 4.6, we define two criteria: • Top three classes (concepts) for each topic
According to these criteria, we select the terms to be linked to the corresponding concepts by the property involve term.

For the related to concept property, we analyze the CTM topic network to extract the relations between concepts. From Table 2, we consider the correlations with 0.10 and above, and from Table 5, we consider the relations of the three assigned classes of the first topic to the three assigned classes of the second topic. The resulting relations between concepts are listed in Table 7. 17 Human Behavior and Emerging Technologies hbet, 2024, 1, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1155/2024/7914028 by University Of Chicago Library, Wiley Online Library on [05/02/2026]. As a result, the created Excel file includes the following columns: ideology, concept, second level concept, third level concept, first concept and second concept (for "Related to Concept" relation), concept to term (for "Involve Term" relation), and term. We use the OntoText Refine 10 annotation tool to map each column to its corresponding class and property from the DarkOnto scheme stored in GraghDB. Figure 10 depicts the DarkOnto full scheme in GraphDB.
Figure 11 demonstrates the sizes of relationships generated among ideology, concept, term classes.

Figure 12 demonstrates the full graph of the ideology and concept instances with their hasTopConcept relations, Figure 13 demonstrates the hasTopConcept relation graph, and Figure 14 shows the concepts with the relatedToConcept relations. The full DarkOnto graph is presented in Figure 15.

4.9. Ontology Evaluation. Following the approach suggested by [50] described in Section 3.7, we consider two major aspects in ontology evaluation, validation and verification. 4.9.1. Verification. Verification refers to the process applied to ensure that the ontology is constructed correctly and its definitions align with the design requirements and function properly to manifest the desired real-world representation [77].

We checked and validated DarkOnto using the W3C RDF Validation Service 11 and OntOlogy Pitfall Scanner 12 (OOPS!) [78]. Moreover, we estimated some metrics about the components of the ontology using OntoMetrics 13 . Table 8 demonstrates various statistics about the constructed ontology.

From the above table, we highlight the expressivity of the ontology scheme through the relationship richness, which is 1.0, and the knowledge delivered by the ontology components through class richness, which is 1.0.

Relationship richness is the ratio of the noninheritance relationships to the total number of the relationships (inheritance and noninheritance) and calculated as in Equation ( 4) [79], where P is the number of noninheritance relationships (P = 4) and H is the number of inheritance relationships. The diversity of relationships in a scheme is an indication of the richness of the scheme [80].

4.9.2. Validation. Validation is the process applied to ensure that the constructed ontology is the right ontology and captures the meaning of the specific real world for which the ontology was built [77].

As this is the first ontology constructed for dark web discussion concepts following an original conceptual design, we manually created a gold standard ontology and then compare it with DarkOnto according to the precision, recall, and F1-score measures [27]. Consequently, we compare the two ontologies through competency questions (CQs) that reflect possible scenarios for four different users and general usage taking into account the defined relations between concepts.

paragraphs: From Table 10, 11 out of 18 questions provided notable F1 -score in the range of 0.74-0.88, with high precision or recall or both. Three questions provided a good F1-score above 0.55. Only four questions resulted in scores below 0.55, with a range of 0.47-0.51.
To look thoroughly at each group of results, we discuss the particularity of precision and recall of each question.

For LEA_CQ1, LEA_CQ2, and LEA_CQ5, high recall percentages were achieved at 0.94, 0.85, and 0.87, respectively, with good precision percentages of 0.66, 0.65, and 0.66, respectively. The high recall indicates a high matching of technical terms in the subjects of networking, security, and encryption, business terms in the subjects of products and services, and terms corresponding to drugs and drug trade, where most of the statements that are supposed to be retrieved were retrieved correctly, while some unrelated statements were retrieved with moderate precision.

LEA_CQ3 provided a moderate recall of 0.59 but with a low precision of 0.44, which may indicate a mediocre matching of terms related to the subject of counterfeit items, with a notable amount of unrelated statements. On the other hand, for LEA_CQ4, the common terms between counterfeit items and drugs were retrieved perfectly with a precision of 1.00; however, a considerable portion of the statements was missing with a recall of 0.35. This result came naturally in the light of the detected relations between concepts depicted in Table 7 and Figure 14, where there is no relation between the two concepts. This inference leads to the need for further research to enhance the relation detection process among concepts.

For SOC_CQ1, common terms between knowledge sharing and opinion sharing were retrieved correctly with high values of both precision and recall of 0.86 and 0.89, respectively. For SOC_CQ2, a good precision of 0.63 was achieved with a low recall of 0.43. Most of the missing terms were slang or terms related to the dark web speech pattern, such as thieve, card fraud, bestmarket profile, good dude, jabber, and rat (which may mean a betrayer, a liar, or just a hateful person 14 ). This result indicates the need to consider a dictionary of slang and uncommon terms to enhance speech patterns. On the other hand, SOC_CQ3 showed a good recall of 0.67 with half of the precision, or 0.50. Notably, all the terms that were retrieved by the system but not considered common in the subjects of state of mind and emotion by the expert were related to the term quality, which are high quality, judge quality, quality, and quality product.

All the criminologists' questions served very well. CRM_ CQ1 achieved a moderate precision of 0.67 and a high recall of 0.96. All other three questions, CRM_CQ2, CRM_CQ3, and CRM_CQ4, provided both high precision of 0.87, 0.92, and 0.87, respectively, and recall of 0.81, 0.79, and 0.82, respectively. These results indicate that the system provided a high match in finding common terms between the subject of consumer mind and drugs, a high match for profit, a high match for Profession, and a high match for common terms between Marketing and Profit.

Similarly, the psychologists' questions served well. PSY_ CQ1 provided good precision at 0.56 and high recall at 0.77, indicating a good match of terms for the emotion subject. Again, the extra statements defined by the system were related to the term quality, which are high quality, judge quality, quality, and quality product, but were not considered by the expert. PSY_CQ2 achieved high percentages for both precision and recall at 0.83 and 0.74, respectively, indicating a high match for the concepts related to acquainting and emotional ideologies, which are knowledge sharing, opinion sharing, state of mind, and emotion. Lastly, PSY_CQ3 achieved a high precision of 0.83 and a good recall of 0.55, indicating a high match of the relation between satisfaction and drugs, whether it was about the drug substances (such as ketamine and heroin) or the drug trade (such as promptly safe, quality product, and good trade) that causes the satisfaction. However, some terms were still missing in the satisfaction aspect in common with drugs, such as suboxone, cocaine, and nbome, causing moderate recall.

Finally, the generic question in its three cases, GN_CQ1_ 1, GN_CQ1_2, and GN_CQ1_3, regarding the terms amphetamine weed, pgp key, and high quality achieved perfect recall at 1.00. GN_CQ1_1 and GN_CQ1_3 provided high and good precision at 0.78 and 0.69, respectively, while GN_CQ1_2 provided low precision at 0.31. This result might be due to the specific condition of some terms (such as pgp), which are highly technical and abbreviated; thus, the system does not define their relations by their exact meaning but with their second bi-gram term (such as key and message). This inference indicates the need to expand the dictionary to include highly technical terms with their abbreviations and acronyms. For example, pgp means Pretty Good Privacy; however, it was not found in the topics in its whole form, but rather with only its acronym pgp, or bigram usage pgp key and pgp message. This finding can be explained by the fact that such terms are very conventional and widely used by members of the forum, or the dark web in general, that we might not find them used in their complete words.

paragraphs: Efficient text analysis is highly dependent on the preprocessing measures taken to clean and refine the textual data, which need thoughtful decisions in determining the proper text cleaning and preprocessing methods. In particular, textual data from social networks is unlike authored text bodies, such as books or articles, which add more challenges to text processing. Such data include a diversity of term usages and In future directions, we will add developments at the concept-relation detection level in topic modeling to enhance identifying the relationships between different concepts. At the OL level, we will add dictionaries to expand the lexical detection of slang and highly technical terms and their acronyms, in addition to synonyms and antonyms, which all serve in incremental OL. Moreover, more hierarchal levels will be added to the ideologies and concepts for more generalized and distinctive definitions.

sections.paper_id: Basheer and Alkhatib - 2024 - DarkOnto An Ontology Construction Approach for Dark Web Community Discussions Through Topic Modelin
paragraphs: The intellectual content of the dark web is fraught with elements corresponding to malicious and illicit activities, in addition to the related topics that these activities need to prosper, including social networking and sharing of knowledge, advice, and viewpoints. The nature of discussions, freedom of speech, data profusion, and the diversity of ideologies and purposes conveyed in these discussions make dark web social networks a substantial data source for studying online crime communities.

This study is aimed at analyzing the intellectual factors of the emergence of criminal groups in terms of terminological components of discussions in the dark web communities, in addition to facilitating the cooperation of law enforcement agencies, sociologists, criminologists, psychologists, and others to take preactive and proactive investigations using a unified resource. The study employed semantic analysis methods through topic modeling using CTM to define the relationships among the semantics within the discussions, which helps infer the knowledge content of the community and understand the formation of the community intellectually and socially. This knowledge content is transformed into a terminological ontology, DarkOnto, using OL methodology. We presented our approach starting from a comprehensive background and discussing thoroughly each phase. These phases include conceptual design, data cleaning and preprocessing, topic modeling, class-topic similarity estimation, ontology construction, ontology population, and ontology evaluation through verification and validation. We introduced possible querying scenarios to compare the coverage and sensitivity of DarkOnto. These scenarios demonstrated the goal of the DarkOnto knowledge graph, and they achieved efficient results.

This study emphasizes the significance of exploring dark web communities to comprehend the evolution of intellectual societies flourishing within the realm of the dark web. Such an exploration can reveal insights into various facets of organized crime that is emerging from the physical world to the virtual world increasingly. This approach is not restricted to cryptomarkets' communities but can be applied to any criminal community, providing an avenue for researching and conceptualizing a diversity of crime zones.
